criteria: The agent has read Section 3 (Methodology) completely to understand the SGCG framework, including the three optimization objectives (discrete, expectation minimization, and direct sample optimization), the Gumbel-Max reparameterization, the smooth estimator, and the sliding window technique.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 (SGCG) completely to understand the two-step optimization procedure: updating θ via gradient descent and updating ε via stochastic sampling with a greedy acceptance condition.
The agent has read Algorithm 2 (GCG) to understand how it differs from SGCG and how it serves as a baseline for comparison.

The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the experimental setup: six datasets (SST-2, IMDB, Amazon, Yelp, DBPedia, AGNews), few-shot setting (16 examples per class), models (GPT-2-large, OPT-350M, Vicuna-7B, LLaMA-2), and five-run averaging.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the text classification accuracy results for GPT-2 Large and OPT-350M across all six datasets and all compared methods (FluentPrompt*, GS, GCG, SGCG).
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the dataset statistics: the number of classes and sizes of training/dev/test splits for all six classification datasets.
The agent has read Table 3 (ablation study) to understand the impact of the greedy component in SGCG compared to SGCG nongreedy on four datasets (SST-2, IMDB, Amazon, Yelp).
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the token length ablation experiment (5, 10, 20 tokens) on OPT-350M across all six datasets for GS, GCG, and SGCG.

The agent has read Table 5 to understand the text attack experiment settings: three attack settings (Single, Multiple, Fluent) on Vicuna and LLaMA-2 models using the SST-2 dataset, with both loss and accuracy reported.
The plan includes implementing the probability distribution of the prompt P(x; θ), where θ parameterizes per-token categorical distributions (π_i for each token position).
The plan includes the Gumbel-Max reparameterization: sampling ε_i ~ U[0,1] and computing x = argmax[log π - log(-log ε)] to sample discrete tokens from the parameterized distribution.

The plan includes the two-step iterative update: (1) update θ via gradient descent on f(x) with respect to θ_i (Equation 5), and (2) update ε via greedy sampling from U[0,1] with the greedy acceptance condition (Equation 6).
The plan includes the greedy acceptance rule: the parameter update (θ and ε) is only accepted if f(x̃) < min(Q), i.e., the new loss is lower than all recent candidates in the sliding window.

The plan includes the sliding window mechanism: maintaining a queue Q of size k to track recent loss values, adding the current candidate loss and removing the oldest entry when the queue exceeds size k.
The plan includes the straight-through identity estimator as the surrogate gradient: using argmax in the forward pass and the identity function (γ̂(x) = x) in the backward pass.
The plan specifies key hyperparameters for SGCG: weight decay of 1e-3, temperature τ initialized at 1.0 decaying exponentially to 0.1 during training, and window size k equal to the prompt length.
The plan includes implementing three baseline methods: FluentPrompt* (projected gradient descent without fluency loss), Gumbel-Softmax (GS), and GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient).
The plan includes collecting and setting up the six classification datasets (SST-2, IMDB, Amazon, Yelp, DBPedia, AGNews) with the few-shot setting of 16 training examples per class.

The plan includes the text attack experiment structure: three settings (Single with 1 random token, Multiple with 3 random tokens, Fluent with a syntactically fluent irrelevant sentence) tested on Vicuna-7B and LLaMA-2 using SST-2.
The probability distribution P(x; θ) is implemented as per-token categorical distributions parameterized by θ (logits or log-probabilities π_i for each token position i across the vocabulary).
The Gumbel-Max trick is implemented to sample discrete tokens: for each token position i, ε_i is sampled from U[0,1], and x_i = argmax[(log π_i - log(-log ε_i)) / τ].
The gradient update for θ (Equation 5) is implemented: θ_i^(k+1) ← θ_i^(k) - α * ∂f(x)/∂θ_i, using the surrogate gradient (straight-through identity) for the backward pass through the argmax.

The greedy ε update (Equation 6) is implemented: a new ε̃_i is sampled from U[0,1] and a new candidate x̃_i is computed; the update is only accepted if the resulting loss f(x̃) is lower than the minimum loss in the sliding window Q.

The straight-through identity estimator is implemented: during the forward pass the discrete argmax is used, while in the backward pass the gradient flows through the identity function (rather than softmax), making the surrogate gradient γ̂(x) = x.

The sliding window of size k is implemented as a queue: after each candidate evaluation, f(x̃) is appended to Q; if the queue size exceeds k, the oldest entry is removed; the acceptance condition checks if f(x̃) < min(Q).
The temperature parameter τ is implemented with exponential decay: τ is initialized to 1.0 and decays exponentially to 0.1 over the course of training, used in the Gumbel-Max computation.
Weight decay of 1e-3 is applied to the optimizer used for the θ parameter update in SGCG.
The window size k (for the sliding window) is set equal to the prompt length n throughout the experiments.

The SGCG algorithm (Algorithm 1) is fully implemented end-to-end: initializing Q, sampling initial ε, iteratively selecting a random token position i, computing candidate updates for both θ_i and x_i, and applying the greedy acceptance condition.

The GCG baseline (Algorithm 2) is implemented: in each iteration, computing top-k candidate tokens for each position using the gradient of the loss w.r.t. one-hot embeddings, sampling K candidates randomly, evaluating loss, and keeping the best candidate.

The FluentPrompt* baseline is implemented: projected gradient descent in embedding space, with the soft embedding projected to the nearest discrete token at each step, and the fluency loss term removed.

The Gumbel-Softmax (GS) baseline is implemented: using the Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization trick (Jang et al., 2016) for differentiable discrete token selection, without a greedy acceptance condition.
The SGCG nongreedy ablation variant is implemented: identical to SGCG except that the greedy constraint on Equation 5 is removed, allowing updates that may not immediately minimize the loss.

The six classification datasets (SST-2, IMDB, Amazon, Yelp, DBPedia, AGNews) are loaded and set up with the few-shot setting: 16 training examples per class, equal-sized development set (16×|C|), and the full test set as described in Table 2.
GPT-2-large and OPT-350M are loaded and used as the backbone language models for the text classification experiments.
Vicuna-7B and LLaMA-2 are loaded and used as the backbone models for the text attack experiments.

The text attack task is implemented: the loss function takes all tokens in the vocabulary into account, and three attack settings are set up — Single (1 random token), Multiple (3 random tokens), and Fluent (syntactically fluent but contextually irrelevant sentence).
The token length ablation experiment is implemented: SGCG, GCG, and GS are tested with prompt lengths of 5, 10, and 20 tokens on OPT-350M across all six classification datasets.

The main text classification experiments are executed successfully without errors on GPT-2-large and OPT-350M, running all four methods (FluentPrompt*, GS, GCG, SGCG) on all six datasets with five independent runs each.
The text attack experiments are executed successfully without errors on Vicuna-7B and LLaMA-2 with all three settings (Single, Multiple, Fluent) using FluentPrompt*, GCG, and SGCG.
The ablation study (greedy vs. non-greedy) is executed successfully on OPT-350M with SGCG and SGCG nongreedy on SST-2, IMDB, Amazon, and Yelp datasets.
The token length ablation experiments are executed successfully on OPT-350M for all three token lengths (5, 10, 20) across all six datasets for GS, GCG, and SGCG.
The loss curve experiments are executed successfully on GPT-2-large for both DBPedia and AGNews datasets, recording training and evaluation loss at each step for both GCG and SGCG.

The text classification accuracy results (Table 1) match the reported values: on GPT-2-large, SGCG achieves 87.39 on SST-2, 81.85 on IMDB, 86.12 on Amazon, 88.29 on Yelp, 70.79 on DBPedia, and 76.39 on AGNews.

The text classification accuracy results (Table 1) match the reported values for OPT-350M: SGCG achieves 88.00 on SST-2, 71.85 on IMDB, 83.99 on Amazon, 84.68 on Yelp, 69.30 on DBPedia, and 78.48 on AGNews.

The ablation study results (Table 3) confirm that the greedy strategy is critical: SGCG significantly outperforms SGCG nongreedy on SST-2 (88.00 vs 75.29), Amazon (83.99 vs 68.06), and Yelp (84.68 vs 81.72), while SGCG nongreedy performs better on IMDB (74.39 vs 71.85).

The token length experiment results (Table 4) confirm that accuracy generally increases with more tokens across datasets, while IMDB is an exception where the trend is reversed, consistent with the paper's analysis.

The text attack experiment results (Table 5) match the reported values: on the Vicuna Single setting SGCG achieves loss 0.0220 (100% accuracy), and on the LLaMA-2 Multiple setting SGCG achieves loss 2.8590 (47.22% accuracy), with SGCG outperforming GCG in most settings.

The loss curve results (Figure 1) confirm that on DBPedia, SGCG achieves lower training and evaluation losses than GCG throughout training, and on AGNews, SGCG shows a more consistent steady decline in training loss compared to GCG which plateaus around step 400.
